title: Justice Will Always Triumph; It Will Certainly Triumph!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored; courtroom exchanges remain explicitly attributed to the letter’s account; and clear OCR character, numeral, punctuation and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The appeal in Jayalalithaa's Rs 66-crore disproportionate-assets case began on 5 January 2015. Arguments before the Karnataka High Court concluded at lightning speed, and yesterday the judge reserved judgment without specifying a date. Before the verdict, this letter recalls the observations Justice C.R. Kumaraswamy made while intervening during the thirty-eight days of hearing.
On 16 February, Jayalalithaa's counsel P. Kumar called it “a false case filed for political revenge.”

Justice Kumaraswamy intervened: “You call it a false case hundreds of times with every breath. But how can I accept that oral assertion when you have not fully produced evidence establishing that the allegations against the accused are untrue? The prosecution examined 259 witnesses and filed 2,341 documents to substantiate its complaint. The accused examined only ninety-nine witnesses and filed 385 documents. Even those do not contain evidence of substance to defeat the prosecution case.”

When Kumar said Jayalalithaa accepted only one rupee as salary while Chief Minister, the judge said: “Even if a Chief Minister takes a salary of one rupee, she remains a public servant. When all are equal before the law, the Vigilance and Anti-Corruption Department has established that Jayalalithaa, who drew a one-rupee salary, possessed income of Rs 66 crore. You have provided no evidence denying it.”

During discussion of Sudhakaran's wedding expenditure, the judge asked: “Was Sudhakaran an ordinary AIADMK cadre or Jayalalithaa's son that so many crores were spent on such a magnificent wedding? You have argued for twenty-eight days and accuse the Special Court judge of giving an erroneous judgment, yet in twenty days you have not established what was wrong with it. What can I do? The situation now requires me to confirm the Special Court judgment.

“You have no evidence denying assets worth Rs 66 crore. Give evidence necessary for your argument; otherwise, I shall appoint an auditor and verify the accounts myself. Even Special Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh's argument will be unnecessary. I shall audit it and deliver judgment. I see no other way.”

When former judge Sudhandhiram began arguing for Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi, Justice Kumaraswamy said: “Sections 120B, criminal conspiracy, and 109, abetment, are central to this case. The Tamil Nadu Vigilance and Anti-Corruption Department alleged that Jayalalithaa used money earned while holding public office to conspire with Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi to purchase properties, and that the three abetted her offences. No one has argued this properly. Address Sections 120B and 109.”

Sudhandhiram replied: “Sections 120B and 109 do not come within Section 13(1)(e) of the Prevention of Corruption Act. The Tamil Nadu police have proved no evidence that Accused No. 1 Jayalalithaa joined a conspiracy.”

The judge said: “Why are you trying to protect Jayalalithaa? Argue only the charges against your clients. Counsel for Accused No. 1 and Accused No. 2, Sasikala, did not cite witness evidence. At least you must use evidence to argue that your clients neither conspired nor abetted an offence.”
Advocate Manishankar, appearing for Sasikala, said: “Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi were punished as abettors of Jayalalithaa's offence without any basic evidence.”

The judge responded: “How could the Special Court have convicted people who did not conspire? Could the Tamil Nadu Vigilance police have registered a case without evidence, conducted it for eighteen years, or the Special Court delivered judgment without examining it? Instead of wasting the Court's time on irrelevant speech, prove with your own evidence any errors in the judgment. You have unnecessarily cited more than sixty-five precedents and have not argued even twenty per cent of your case with evidence.”
Sudhandhiram said: “Let Bhavani Singh argue the charges against us; we shall respond.”

The judge said: “Do you know the basis on which criminal cases must be argued? You worry only about Jayalalithaa. Worry about your own petitioners and argue their charges. The judgment will rest on the arguments.”
Kumar said: “Sudhakaran's wedding expenditure of Rs 6.45 crore must be removed from Jayalalithaa's expenditure account.”

The judge replied: “You continue to argue orally. You must prove what evidence and witnesses support your building-renovation expenditure. The Tamil Nadu Vigilance and Anti-Corruption Department has proved eighty-two to ninety-two per cent of the charges with appropriate witnesses and evidence. You have not shown witness evidence and have argued only thirty to thirty-five per cent.”
Kumar said: “Thirty-five marks are enough to pass.”
The judge replied: “Thirty-five marks may pass at school. In court, you pass only by scoring more than the other side. On that basis, they have scored sixty-five marks more than you. They pass.”
**Hearing on 18 February 2015**

Sudhandhiram submitted a list of Sudhakaran's and Ilavarasi's assets during the check period and sought permission for detailed argument. The judge asked: “What justice is there in offering a detailed account now after failing to do so during eighteen years of proceedings? What is in this list?”

Sudhandhiram said Sudhakaran had income of Rs 2,12,47,978 and bought immovable property for Rs 1,10,04,961. When counsel stumbled over the arithmetic, the judge used his own calculator and stated the correct figures.

The judge asked whether the list had been submitted to and certified by the Income Tax Department. Sudhandhiram said no. The judge asked: “Why present here a list never filed with the Department? How did so much property arise during the check period?”

Counsel said Sudhakaran served as a director in several companies and earned income through their business. Super Duper TV had been contracted by the Tamil Nadu Tourism Development Corporation to supply vehicles, projectors and other equipment for exhibitions statewide. It billed Rs 42 lakh and received Rs 39.50 lakh by bank draft, but the Vigilance Department allegedly failed to count it as income.
The judge asked: “When Amma was Chief Minister, was it legally proper to award Government work to a company belonging to her son? Is that not abuse of authority?” Counsel could not answer.

The judge continued: “For twenty-eight days I have insisted that you argue within the Special Court judgment, but you have not taken me seriously. Though told not to ruminate upon old incidents, you continue. Read whatever you wish. In the end, I shall examine the truth and deliver the appropriate judgment.”
**Hearing on 19 February**
Sudhandhiram argued that the Special Court had not questioned Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi about prosecution witness statements and could not rely on unput allegations.

The judge replied: “Ilavarasi was asked around 650 questions based on prosecution evidence. She answered more than ninety-five per cent only with ‘yes’ or ‘no.’ If you doubt it, read her court statement.”

He added: “A judgment cannot rest on oral submissions alone. It must rest on evidence, documents and witnesses. The allegation is that Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi accumulated assets using Jayalalithaa's income while she was Chief Minister. How will you prove that false with evidence? What evidence shows that Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi did not accumulate property unlawfully? You have filed none.”
**Hearing on 20 February**

The judge asked Sudhandhiram whether he had concluded. He said yes. The judge replied: “The hearing has run for thirty-one days. You said the DVAC filed a false case, but you have concluded without providing the evidence I requested.”
Counsel for the companies argued: “The Special Court wrongly held that our companies belonged to the accused and included them in its judgment. They must be removed from the case.”
The judge asked: “Why did you not object when the companies were included? What is the purpose of remaining silent then and asking after eighteen years?” Counsel remained silent.
Justice Kumaraswamy openly told Jayalalithaa's lawyers: “If you keep speaking without producing proper documents, I may have to give the same judgment as Kunha.”
Jayalalithaa's counsel Nageswara Rao said gifts received by her formed part of her income, were declared to the Income Tax Department, and had been accepted by the Special Court.

The judge intervened: “The Income Tax Department's purpose is only to accept a person's declaration of assets, determine the tax and collect it. Under the Prevention of Corruption Act, however, authorities may ask what property was accumulated and how. Why repeatedly cite Income Tax Appellate Tribunal rulings? Focus on what injustice occurred in the judgment concerning Rs 66 crore in assets. Do not waste the Court's time.”
**Hearing on 29 January 2015**

Addressing Sasikala's counsel, the judge said: “Prepare all necessary information before arguing. Your allegation is untrue. Read the statements of prosecution witnesses, including Canara Bank officer Arunachalam. Then you will understand that your information is wrong.”
Counsel said: “Jayalalithaa and Sasikala have no connection to the allegation of disproportionate assets.”
The judge replied: “Do not make a vague denial. The case has run for eighteen years. Both are partners in Jaya Publications and Sasi Enterprises. The accused themselves admit it.”
Counsel repeated that the case was political revenge.

The judge said: “The Special Court judge, after a full trial, tied 150 knots in the judgment establishing wrongdoing. In appeal, you must untie each knot with proper evidence. None of you has attempted it or shown that evidence.”
Sasikala's counsel Basant said media reports should not determine judgment and claimed the lower court ruled without evidence.

The judge replied: “The Special Court judge examined 65,000 pages of documents, questioned 252 witnesses, heard both sides and wrote a thousand-page judgment after complete analysis. How could punishment have been imposed without evidence?

“You have argued for twenty-eight days and accuse the lower-court judge of error. Yet you have not established with evidence that the charges he sustained were wrong. What can I do? The situation requires me to confirm Judge Kunha's judgment. You have no evidence that Jayalalithaa did not acquire Rs 66 crore in assets. Stop wasting time with oral submissions and provide evidence. Otherwise, I shall appoint an auditor, verify the accounts and deliver judgment without even requiring Bhavani Singh's argument. I see no other way.
“You say the Special Court ignored your side. Yet from Rs 13 crore in building expenditure, it deducted twenty per cent in your favour.”
Sudhandhiram said Sudhakaran's total assets were Rs 2,12,47,978.
The judge said: “Very well, provide the Income Tax Department document. Does your figure agree with its assessment?”
Sudhandhiram said no.
The judge replied: “Then why give me these figures? You must file the same accounts filed before the Special Court. I shall not accept these.”

Udanpirappē, I have compiled here the successive observations attributed in the hearings to Karnataka High Court Justice C.R. Kumaraswamy: that the defence produced no evidence establishing the complaint to be false; that the Vigilance Department had established assets of Rs 66 crore for Jayalalithaa, who claimed a one-rupee salary; that circumstances might require confirmation of the Special Court judgment; that despite citing more than sixty-five precedents, the defence had not argued even twenty per cent with evidence; that he would finally examine the truth and judge accordingly; that no document disproving the charge had been filed; that the defence concluded without supplying evidence for its claim that the DVAC case was false; that the prosecution examined 259 witnesses and filed 2,341 supporting documents; and that he might have to give the same judgment as Judge Kunha.
The verdict will come within days. Let us wait. Justice will always triumph; it will certainly triumph. No shortcut can prevent it.
With affection,
M.K.
13 March 2015
